deck_meta.deck_audience: Enterprise leaders, HR strategists, AI deployment teams

slides.title: Generative AI at Work
slides.subtitle: Evidence from 5,172 Customer-Service Agents on the Real Productivity Impact of GenAI
slides.footer_meta: Brynjolfsson, Li & Raymond · NBER WP 31161 / arXiv:2304.11771v2 · 2024

slides.title: Why This Study Matters
slides.key_message: First rigorous, large-scale, causal evidence of how GenAI changes real workplaces.

points: Outage natural experiment for learning persistence

items.label: Findings & Trends
items.sub: Headline numbers and the dispersion behind them

items.label: Themes
items.sub: Compressed learning curves and customer experience

items.label: Insights
items.sub: Two structural risks lurking behind the win

items.sub: Five concrete moves for enterprise leaders

slides.title: 01  Findings & Trends
preview: But the gain is wildly uneven

slides.title: The Headline Numbers
slides.key_message: AI lifts average productivity by 15%, but the gains are deeply uneven.

stats.label: Avg. resolutions per hour (RPH) across 5,172 agents
RPH gain for the lowest-skill quintile
RPH gain for the highest-skill quintile
stats.context: With small declines in quality metrics

stats.label: Reduction in new-hire attrition (≤6 mo. tenure)

slides.narrative: Average gain masks a striking distribution: junior agents leap forward, senior agents barely move. The same intervention, very different outcomes — and that asymmetry is the entire story.

slides.title: The Productivity Lift Is Not Evenly Shared
slides.key_message: Gains concentrate at the bottom; the top barely moves.
slides.source_note: §5.2; Table 3 — RPH gain by pre-treatment skill quintile (illustrative; intermediate quintile values approximated from Figures 4–5).

slides.title: Thesis: GenAI Is Skill-Equalizing
slides.primary_quote: Less experienced and lower-skilled workers improve both the speed and quality of their output while the most experienced and highest-skilled workers see small gains in speed and small declines in quality.
slides.secondary_quote: Treated agents with two months of tenure perform just as well as untreated agents with more than six months of tenure.
slides.implication: This is the first major IT wave that does not behave as skill-biased technical change. The historical pattern — where new tech rewarded the strong and widened inequality — is reversed.

slides.title: Three Mechanisms Behind the Gains
slides.key_message: The lift isn't magic — it's encoded best practice flowing top → bottom.

columns.body: AI learns top performers' patterns and broadcasts them to everyone — durable learning, not real-time dependency.
columns.evidence: During AI service outages, previously-treated agents still outperform their pre-AI baseline. Learning persists.

columns.body: Conversation analysis shows non-native English speakers' fluency converging toward top-performer style.
columns.evidence: Linguistic features of low-skill agents shift measurably toward those of high-skill peers post-AI exposure.

columns.body: The largest gains are on rare issues — where the human baseline is weakest, AI net contribution is highest.
columns.evidence: Counter-intuitive: AI has less training data on rare topics, but human baseline is even worse, so net help is greatest.

slides.title: Theme A — The Learning Curve Is Compressed
slides.key_message: Ramp-up time collapses; tenure-based seniority loses its signal value.

points: Senior agents 2× as productive as new hires

callout.label: Strategic implication
callout.text: Traditional 'pay-for-experience' compensation curves were designed for a world where tenure = capability. AI breaks that proxy.

slides.title: Theme B — Customer Experience Improves Too
slides.key_message: AI assistance doesn't make agents feel robotic — customers respond better.

callout.label: Why this is novel
callout.text: First large-scale evidence that AI augmentation can improve the experience of work itself — not just throughput.

slides.title: Strategic Insight: Two Long-Term Risks
slides.key_message: Today's productivity win quietly creates two structural problems.

cards.body: If senior agents increasingly defer to AI's median-quality suggestions, their original creative contribution declines.
cards.evidence: Top quintile shows small declines in quality metrics (resolution rate, NPS) even as speed marginally rises.
cards.implication: → The very signal future AI models depend on starts to weaken at the source.

cards.body: Agent conversations are simultaneously customer service AND model training data — but employees are not compensated for the latter.
cards.evidence: "Whether and how workers should be compensated for the data they provide for training AI systems" is left as an open policy question.
cards.implication: → Without attribution mechanisms, the source of intelligence erodes over time.

slides.title: 05  Recommended Actions
slides.kicker: What enterprise leaders should do now

slides.title: Five Moves for Enterprise Leaders
slides.key_message: Treat this paper as an operating playbook, not a forecast.

actions.what: Re-evaluate seniority-based pay curves — AI compresses the experience premium.
actions.outcome: Pay-for-skill replaces pay-for-tenure; reduces overpaying for time served.

actions.what: Make new-hire retention a hard KPI for AI deployments, not just speed metrics.
actions.outcome: −40% attrition has more durable ROI than +15% throughput.

actions.what: Establish attribution & incentive systems for employees whose work trains AI models.
actions.outcome: Protects long-term training data quality; aligns incentives with model health.

actions.what: Use AI to shrink customer-service ramp-up from 6–9 months to 2–3 months.
actions.outcome: Faster time-to-productivity; lower cost-to-train per agent.

actions.what: Reserve high-difficulty, AI-free tasks for senior staff to preserve creative output.
actions.outcome: Prevents flattening; preserves the 'human signal' future models need.

slides.title: The Big Picture
slides.key_message: GenAI is the first IT wave that lifts the bottom — but it asks new questions of the top.

pillars.body: AI shifted from skill-amplifier to skill-equalizer. The 5,172-agent dataset is the strongest causal evidence to date.
Tenure-based seniority loses signal value. New-hire ramp-up compresses. Customer experience gets better, not worse.
Top-talent flattening and training-data decay are slow-moving risks. They don't show up in this year's KPIs.

slides.closing_line: The window to redesign HR economics, ROI targets, and data-attribution mechanisms is now — while the gains are still accruing.

items: Brynjolfsson, E., Li, D., & Raymond, L. (2024). Generative AI at Work. NBER Working Paper No. 31161 / arXiv:2304.11771v2 (Nov 7, 2024).
Published as: Quarterly Journal of Economics, Vol. 140, No. 2, 2025, pp. 889–942.
Sample: 5,172 customer-service agents at a Fortune 500 business-process software firm.
Identification: Two-way fixed effects DiD; Sun-Abraham robust estimators.
Natural experiment: AI service outages used to test learning persistence.
